title: On Being Bound to Be True

ast.value: On Being Bound to Be True
reflective public snippet lincoln integrity writing

ast: I am not bound to win, but I am bound to be true.
What makes this quote stick is what it does

say. Almost every framing of honesty or integrity eventually ties back to outcome - be truthful and you will earn trust, do the right thing and it pays off eventually. Lincoln cuts that thread entirely. You are not even bound to be right. Just true. The decoupling is the whole point.

For anyone in software, this lands differently than it might seem. There is constant pressure to project certainty: confident estimates, definitive diagnoses, strong opinions delivered without hedging. The quote gives permission to say "I do not know" without treating that as a failure mode. You do not have to win the argument. You just cannot lie about what you actually see.

The word "bound" is doing quiet work here too. Lincoln is not describing a virtue he aspires to or a goal he is working toward. He is describing a constraint he operates under. That is a fundamentally different posture - not honesty as character development, but honesty as load-bearing structure. Something you cannot remove without the whole thing collapsing.
